title: Introduction to Pashto
description: An overview of the Pashto language — its origins, unique sounds, and script.
author: Poha Editorial

content.heading: What is Pashto?
lines: Pashto (پښتو) is an Eastern Iranian language. It is one of the two official languages of Afghanistan and the second-largest regional language of Pakistan. It is the native and historical tongue of the Pashtun people.
Unlike Persian, Pashto has preserved many ancient phonological features of the Indo-Iranian language family, making it uniquely rich in its sound structure.

content.text: Pashto is known for its distinct retroflex consonants (sounds made by curling the tongue back), which are not found in standard Persian or Arabic.

content.heading: The Alphabet & Script
lines: Pashto is written in a modified Perso-Arabic script, read from right to left. The Pashto alphabet consists of 44 letters.
It includes specialized letters to represent its unique sounds, such as ټ (tt), ډ (dd), ړ (rr), ږ (zh), and ښ (sh/kh).

content.heading: Essential Greetings
lines: Pashtun culture places a heavy emphasis on warm, elaborate greetings. The most common daily greeting is 'Staray mashe' (ستړی مه شې), which literally translates to 'May you not be tired.'
Other essential phrases include: 'Pakhair raghlay' (په خیر راغلئ) — Welcome; 'Manana' (مننه) — Thank you; and 'De khuday pa aman' (د خدای په امان) — Goodbye.

content.question: What does the common Pashto greeting 'Staray mashe' literally mean?
content.explanation: 'Staray mashe' (ستړی مه شې) means 'May you not be tired.' It is a deeply ingrained greeting acknowledging the hard work and resilience of the person you are meeting.

content.heading: Next Steps
lines: In the next lesson, you will explore the historical foundation of the Pashtun people and the empire that shaped modern Afghanistan.
Continue to Lesson 2 when you are ready!
